data.title: Common English Words
data.description: Learn and practice everyday English vocabulary

questions.questionText: What is the opposite of 'big'?
questions.explanation: The opposite of 'big' is 'small'. Tiny is also small but 'small' is the most common opposite.

questions.questionText: I drink ___ every morning. (coffee/tea/water)
questions.explanation: Common breakfast drinks include coffee, tea, water, milk, or juice.

questions.questionText: Which word means 'happy'?
questions.explanation: Joyful means very happy. Sad is the opposite of happy.

questions.questionText: The ___ is shining today. (sun/moon/star)
questions.explanation: During the day, the sun shines. At night, the moon and stars shine.

questions.questionText: A 'book' is something you read.
questions.explanation: Correct! A book is made of pages with text that you read.

data.title: Present Perfect Tense Mastery
data.description: Learn when to use present perfect and simple past

questions.questionText: I ___ to London three times.
questions.explanation: Use 'have been' for life experiences without a specific time. 'Went' needs a specific time (I went to London last year).

questions.questionText: She ___ (finish) her homework already.
questions.explanation: Use 'has finished' with 'already' to show a completed action. Present perfect = has/have + past participle.

questions.questionText: They ___ each other since 2010.
questions.explanation: Use present perfect with 'since' for actions that started in the past and continue now.

questions.questionText: Present perfect uses 'have/has + past participle'.
questions.explanation: Correct! Present perfect structure: have/has + past participle (e.g., have eaten, has gone).

questions.questionText: I ___ (see) that movie yesterday.
questions.explanation: Use simple past 'saw' with specific time words like 'yesterday'. NOT 'have seen' because we know when.

data.title: Understanding Climate Change
data.description: Read and analyze a text about environmental issues
data.content: Climate change represents one of the most pressing challenges of our time. Rising global temperatures, caused primarily by greenhouse gas emissions, are leading to more frequent extreme weather events, rising sea levels, and disruptions to ecosystems worldwide. While scientific consensus is clear about the human causes of climate change, public discourse remains fragmented. The complexity of climate systems, coupled with economic and political considerations, creates a multifaceted problem requiring interdisciplinary solutions. Renewable energy, sustainable agriculture, and international cooperation are essential components of any comprehensive strategy to address this global crisis.

questions.questionText: What is the main cause of climate change according to the passage?
questions.explanation: The passage states 'caused primarily by greenhouse gas emissions'.

questions.questionText: The passage describes public discourse as ___.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states 'public discourse remains fragmented', meaning divided or not unified.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, there is scientific consensus on the human causes of climate change.
questions.explanation: The passage clearly states 'scientific consensus is clear about the human causes'.

questions.questionText: What type of solutions does the passage suggest are needed?
questions.explanation: The passage mentions 'interdisciplinary solutions', meaning solutions from multiple fields working together.

data.title: At the Restaurant
data.description: Listen to a conversation at a restaurant and answer questions
data.content: Transcript: Waiter: Good evening! Are you ready to order? Customer: Yes, I'd like the chicken pasta, please. Waiter: Would you like a drink with that? Customer: Yes, a glass of water, please. Waiter: Anything else? Customer: No, that's all. Thank you!

questions.questionText: What did the customer order to eat?
questions.explanation: The customer said 'I'd like the chicken pasta, please.'

questions.questionText: The customer ordered a glass of ___.
questions.explanation: The customer said 'a glass of water, please.'

questions.questionText: The customer ordered dessert.
questions.explanation: The customer said 'No, that's all' when asked if they wanted anything else.

data.title: Describe Your Daily Routine
data.description: Write about what you do every day

questions.questionText: Write a paragraph (50-80 words) describing your typical daily routine. Include: what time you wake up, what you do in the morning, afternoon, and evening.
questions.correctAnswer: Sample: I wake up at 7 AM every day. In the morning, I have breakfast and go to work. I work from 9 AM to 5 PM. In the afternoon, I have lunch with my colleagues. In the evening, I go home, have dinner, and watch TV. I go to bed at 11 PM.
questions.explanation: A good daily routine paragraph should include time expressions (in the morning, at 7 AM), present simple tense, and sequence words.

questions.questionText: Which tense should you use to describe daily routines?
questions.explanation: Use Present Simple for habits and routines (I wake up, I go, I have).

questions.questionText: Complete: I ___ (go) to work every day.
questions.explanation: Use base form 'go' with 'I' in present simple for routines.

questions.questionText: Record yourself reading this sentence: 'She sells seashells by the seashore.' Focus on the 'sh' and 's' sounds.
questions.correctAnswer: Manual grading required - teacher will evaluate pronunciation
questions.explanation: The 'sh' sound is made with lips rounded forward. The 's' sound is made with tongue behind teeth. Practice slowly first.

questions.questionText: Which word has a different vowel sound? Listen carefully.
questions.explanation: 'Bit' has a short 'i' sound /ɪ/. The others have a long 'ee' sound /iː/.

questions.questionText: The 'th' sound in 'think' is the same as the 'th' sound in 'this'.
questions.explanation: False! 'Think' has voiceless /θ/, 'this' has voiced /ð/. Put your hand on your throat - 'this' vibrates.

questions.questionText: Describe in 2-3 sentences how you practice English speaking. What methods work best for you?
questions.correctAnswer: Sample: I practice speaking by talking to native speakers online. I also record myself and listen to find mistakes. Watching English movies helps me learn natural pronunciation.
questions.explanation: Good speaking practice includes: conversation practice, self-recording, shadowing native speakers, and watching authentic content.

data.title: Common English Idioms
data.description: Understand and use idiomatic expressions correctly

questions.questionText: What does 'break the ice' mean?
options: To make people feel more comfortable in a social situation
questions.correctAnswer: To make people feel more comfortable in a social situation
questions.explanation: 'Break the ice' means to do or say something to make people feel more relaxed, especially at the start of a meeting or party.

questions.questionText: It's raining cats and ___.
questions.explanation: 'Raining cats and dogs' means raining very heavily. It's a common English idiom.

questions.questionText: If something 'costs an arm and a leg', it means it is:
questions.explanation: 'Cost an arm and a leg' is an idiom meaning something is extremely expensive.

questions.questionText: Don't cry over spilled ___.
questions.explanation: 'Don't cry over spilled milk' means don't worry about things that have already happened and can't be changed.

questions.questionText: 'Piece of cake' means something is very difficult.
questions.explanation: False! 'Piece of cake' means something is very easy, not difficult. Example: 'The test was a piece of cake.'

data.title: Advanced English Proficiency Test
data.description: Test all your English skills at the highest level
data.content: The phenomenon of linguistic relativity, often associated with the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, posits that the structure of a language affects its speakers' cognition and worldview. While strong deterministic interpretations have been largely discredited, contemporary research suggests more nuanced relationships between language and thought. Cross-linguistic studies reveal fascinating variations in how different languages encode concepts such as time, space, and causality, potentially influencing speakers' cognitive processes in subtle yet measurable ways.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the passage?
options: The relationship between language and thought
questions.correctAnswer: The relationship between language and thought
questions.explanation: The passage discusses linguistic relativity - how language structure affects cognition and worldview.

questions.questionText: The passage states that strong deterministic interpretations have been ___.
questions.explanation: 'Discredited' means shown to be false or unreliable. The passage says strong versions of the hypothesis are no longer accepted.

questions.questionText: What does 'nuanced' mean in this context?
questions.explanation: 'Nuanced' means characterized by subtle shades of meaning or expression - complex and not simple.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, different languages encode concepts like time and space in identical ways.
questions.explanation: False! The passage mentions 'fascinating variations in how different languages encode concepts such as time, space, and causality.'

questions.questionText: In your own words, explain what 'linguistic relativity' means based on the passage. (30-50 words)
questions.correctAnswer: Linguistic relativity is the idea that the structure and features of a language can influence how its speakers think and perceive the world. While extreme versions have been rejected, research shows language may subtly affect cognition.
questions.explanation: A good answer should mention: language structure, influence on thought/cognition, and the nuanced nature of this relationship.
